result:
Repo has no `src/edge/` or `config/` — handbook is the spec, not shipped code. Plan targets paths as handbook describes.

1. Per-route body size config — new file `config/body_size_limits.json` keyed by `METHOD route_prefix` → bytes, default 2097152, override `POST /v1/media/upload-meta` → 524288. Mirrors `config/rate_limits.json` shape so ops reviewers recognize it.

2. `body_size_guard` middleware change (`src/edge/pipeline.ts` step 4 / its own file) — lookup limit by `(req.method, matched route prefix)` before reading body. Reject with `413` RFC 7807: `type: '/errors/body-too-large'`, `title: 'Payload Too Large'`, `status: 413`, `detail: 'Limit 524288 bytes exceeded'`, `instance: req.request_id`. No stacktrace, no internal host per Section 2 rule 6.

3. Pipeline ordering preserved — guard stays at step 4 (before `auth_jwt`). DoS defense must not require JWT verify first; don't move it. If route prefix match needs dispatcher, do a lightweight prefix lookup inside the guard (same table as rate_limits), not full dispatcher.

4. Streaming enforcement — enforce via `Content-Length` pre-check AND a streaming byte-counter on the request stream (abort+413 mid-stream). `Content-Length` alone is bypassable by chunked transfer; attackers doing DoS will omit or lie.

5. CODEOWNERS approval — Section 6 lists `src/edge/auth_jwt.ts`, `rate_limit.ts`, `header_sanitize.ts`, `trusted_proxies.json` as gated. `body_size_guard` isn't named, but this is a security-relevant trust-boundary change; request CODEOWNERS add `body_size_guard.ts` and `body_size_limits.json` to the allowlist in the same PR.

6. Unit tests (`src/edge/body_size_guard.test.ts`) — cases: 524287B accepted on `POST /v1/media/upload-meta`, 524289B rejected with exact RFC 7807 body, other routes still accept up to 2097152B, `GET` path unaffected, chunked request exceeding mid-stream aborts with `413`.

7. Integration test (`tests/integration/body_size_media_upload_meta.test.ts`) — hits real Redis+PG per Section 11 rule, posts a real 600KB body to `/v1/media/upload-meta`, asserts `413` + problem+json, asserts no partial forward to `media-svc` (check Envoy egress counter unchanged).

8. Lint / regression guard — add a test that asserts every route in `rate_limits.json` resolves a body_size limit (even if default), so the two configs can't silently drift. Prevents "new route, forgot body limit" regression.

9. Metric — new counter `atlas_edge_body_size_rejections_total{route, method, reason}` where `reason ∈ {content_length, streamed}`. Register in same module as other `atlas_edge_*` metrics so Prometheus scrape picks it up with no config change.

10. Alert — Alertmanager rule: sustained `rate(atlas_edge_body_size_rejections_total{route="/v1/media/upload-meta"}[5m]) > X` → `#oncall-platform` (SEV3, not paging). X set from a week of baseline body-size histogram data — see next item. Alert is for "legit users broken", not for DoS (DoS being blocked is success).

11. Pre-rollout size histogram — before shipping the clamp, add `atlas_edge_request_body_size_bytes{route, method}` histogram (buckets 64KB, 128KB, 256KB, 512KB, 1MB, 2MB) and ship that alone first. Leave it a week; confirm P99 of legit `POST /v1/media/upload-meta` bodies is well under 512KB. If not, raise the limit before step 12 or the rollout creates a user-visible outage.

12. Rollout sequence per Section 6 — canary `us-east-1` 5% for 30 min, soak 10 min, then eu-west-1 full, soak 10 min, then ap-southeast-1 full. Watch the new rejection counter and `atlas_edge_requests_total{route="/v1/media/upload-meta",status_class="4xx"}` at each step. Add route to `config/always_trace_routes.json` for the rollout week so we have 100% traces on rejections for forensics.

13. Rollback — two levers. (a) Cheap: flip the entry in `config/body_size_limits.json` back to 2097152 and Argo-sync; no code revert needed if limits are hot-loaded. Confirm config is hot-reloaded or triggers a pod restart that's fast enough. (b) If code path is buggy, Argo rollback to previous green per region — same SEV1 auto-rollback that already exists covers it.

14. Config reload semantics — verify whether `config/*.json` is read once at boot or watched. If boot-only, rollback via config is a full re-deploy; either add a SIGHUP reload for `body_size_limits.json` now, or document that rollback = revert PR + re-deploy. Don't leave this ambiguous before rollout.

15. Abuse-pattern confirmation — before clamping, pull 7 days of `media-svc` logs for `POST /v1/media/upload-meta` and verify the DoS pattern (bodies well above 512KB from few IPs, or many small IPs rotating). If the abuse is actually small bodies at high rate, clamp fixes nothing — rate-limit rule in `config/rate_limits.json` is the right lever. Don't ship the wrong fix.

16. Comms — (a) `#oncall-platform` announce + link to PR and rollout window. (b) Partner integrations team (API-key users per Section 5): direct email to their engineering contact if any partner uses this route, with 512KB limit and `413` shape. (c) First-party client teams (web + mobile): Slack DM with the new limit and expected `413` body so clients can surface a user-friendly error instead of generic failure. (d) Public API docs update (changelog + route reference) same day as `us-east-1` canary, not earlier (don't advertise a limit you haven't shipped). (e) Security on-call FYI since this closes a DoS vector — ask whether they want forensic capture of rejected requests (bodies are dropped pre-auth so PII risk is low, but confirm).

17. Audit log field — add `rejected_reason: 'body_too_large'` to the Section 3 step 9 audit log line when the guard fires, so the 365-day S3 audit trail has the abuse signal. Redaction per Section 2 rule 5 still applies to any PII header.

Open risk: guard runs before `auth_jwt`, so rejections are anonymous — you can count them by IP (from `trustedClientIp`) but cannot attribute to user/org. That's acceptable for DoS defense; note it in the PR so reviewers don't ask.
